episode_key: 2025-08-30 - Using Google Ads, Heygen AI, Perplexity to make you $$$
title: Using Google Ads, Heygen AI, Perplexity to make you $$$
executive_summary: Cody Schneider delivers a hands-on masterclass for bootstrapped founders on customer acquisition through Google Ads, Facebook Ads, and AI-generated video content. The episode walks through keyword research with Keywords Everywhere, setting up click-to-conversion campaigns on Google, and creating UGC-style Facebook ads using HeyGen avatars and ElevenLabs voices. Key insight: most indie hackers irrationally avoid paid ads despite favorable unit economics, and low search volume niches (100 searches/month) can sustain profitable micro-SaaS businesses.
key_takeaways: Validate demand before building: use keyword research and Reddit threads to confirm people are searching for your solution before writing a single line of code
Start Google Ads with click campaigns first, then graduate to signup conversions, then payment conversions as data accumulates - you need volume to train the algorithm
AI avatar ads (HeyGen + ElevenLabs) combined with Instagram Trial Reels provide a near-zero-cost testing ground for ad creative before committing budget
opportunities: AI avatar ad creation agency serving vibe-coded SaaS products that need customer acquisition
Data extraction micro-SaaS tools targeting niche keyword families with 100-500 monthly searches
quotes: Apps that have a 100 search volume a month, I've paid my rent for the last 10 years. Like I will never talk about these in public.

episode_key: 2025-09-03 - I quit my job to make $6M/year with AI apps
title: I quit my job to make $6M/year with AI apps
executive_summary: Ben, the creator of Wombo (100M+ downloads), shares his playbook for building viral AI-powered mobile apps: identify open-source AI models gaining traction in technical circles, wrap them in dead-simple UIs, and engineer the output for shareability. The conversation covers his journey from zero to 250M installs, surviving a near-fatal funding collapse, and pivoting toward decentralized compute with W.ai. His core thesis: copy what works, put your own spin on it, and optimize for the content creation loop.
key_takeaways: When an AI model goes viral in technical communities but is hard for laypeople to use, wrapping it in a simple mobile app is a proven recipe for massive adoption
Design apps for a viral content creation loop: user creates content, shares it on social platforms, which drives new users back to the app organically
Monetize with weekly subscriptions ($3-$20/week), knowing that 98% of users will be free and drive virality while 2% generate all revenue
opportunities: AI mobile apps that productize trending content formats (e.g., turning celebrities into animated characters, baby-fying photos)
Decentralized compute networks that let consumer devices contribute idle GPU/CPU cycles for AI inference
quotes: If you make something truly great and truly unique and kind of truly captures the current moment, then I think you can get hundreds of millions of downloads and make at least tens of millions of dollars just with popcorn mobile apps.

episode_key: 2025-09-08 - Screensharing How This Guy Makes Millions with AI Agents (Rowan Cheung)
title: Screensharing How This Guy Makes Millions with AI Agents (Rowan Cheung)
executive_summary: Rowan Cheung, founder of The Rundown AI newsletter, reveals seven AI workflows that allow his 15-person team to operate like a 50-person organization. Demonstrations include AI avatar content creation (HeyGen + ElevenLabs), voice-dictated tweet writing with Claude, a Lindy-powered scheduling agent that responds to leads in under 60 seconds, a custom GPT for employee onboarding, and a Zapier agent that filters support emails and routes them to the right team via Slack. The episode provides copy-paste templates for each workflow.
key_takeaways: Train Claude on your top-performing content to create a voice-matched writing assistant - use it as an editor-in-chief rather than a first-draft generator for highest quality output
Deploy an AI scheduling agent (Lindy) that responds to inbound leads within 60 seconds, 24/7 - speed-to-response directly correlates with conversion rates in services businesses
Build a custom GPT loaded with SOPs, HR policies, and team directories to onboard new employees without burdening managers or creating information bottlenecks
opportunities: AI-powered executive assistant services for founders who need 24/7 scheduling, email triage, and lead qualification without hiring human EAs
Template marketplace for Claude/GPT writing styles trained on high-performing content in specific niches
quotes: Sometimes it would be faster if I just came in and quickly narrated it, but I don't even have that 10 minutes of time and that break in the workflow every single day takes a lot of mental capacity.

episode_key: 2025-09-10 - This Solopreneur Called Me to Fix His $20k/mo SaaS
title: This Solopreneur Called Me to Fix His $20k/mo SaaS
executive_summary: A live consulting session where Greg Isenberg and marketer Gabe diagnose the retention problems of PostBridge, a $20K MRR social media scheduling SaaS run by solopreneur Jack Ficks. The analysis reveals five strategic gaps: no community, weak brand positioning, absence from YouTube, no proprietary analytics metric, and limited API/MCP integrations. The episode provides a universally applicable framework for reducing churn through world-building, contextual onboarding, gamification, and converting monthly subscribers to annual plans via email marketing.
key_takeaways: Build a community around your product and include it as a subscription perk - users who connect with other users have dramatically lower churn rates
Create a proprietary metric unique to your product (like VidIQ's 'outlier score') to differentiate in a crowded market and lock users into your ecosystem
Use customer call transcripts fed into ChatGPT to discover messaging patterns and rewrite your positioning using customers' own language
opportunities: Gamification-as-a-service agency that helps SaaS products add streaks, leaderboards, and time-saved metrics to reduce churn
MCP integration layer for scheduling tools enabling agentic posting from AI workflows
quotes: I always look at everything as like what's the MVP to this? Like maybe it's not a new video every week, but maybe it's like once a month or once every two months even, like just something.

episode_key: 2025-09-12 - AI Search is PRINTING (ChatGPT, GEO & Perplexity)
title: AI Search is PRINTING (ChatGPT, GEO & Perplexity)
executive_summary: Cody Schneider breaks down Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) - the practice of getting your brand cited by AI chatbots like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini. He explains the mechanics: AI models fan out user queries into 100+ derivative searches, scrape the top-ranking Google pages, and synthesize answers. Brands that appear on more of these source URLs get cited more often. While conversion rates from AI search traffic are 10-40% (vs. 1-2% from traditional search), total volume remains small, making this a supplement rather than replacement for SEO.
key_takeaways: AI search works by scraping Google pages 1-3 for source material - your job is to get your brand mentioned on as many of those ranking URLs as possible through outreach and link placement
Use AI SEO tracker tools to find which URLs are being referenced for queries related to your product, then systematically contact those site owners to get included
GEO delivers overnight results unlike traditional SEO, but is vulnerable to model updates - treat it as one channel in your stack, not your entire strategy
opportunities: AI search optimization agency that identifies source URLs, manages outreach campaigns, and tracks brand mention percentages across LLMs
Listicle placement marketplace connecting brands seeking AI search visibility with publishers ranking on relevant queries
quotes: If organic search is working for you, paid search will work for you. I guarantee it. 99 out of 100 times it will work. It's a skill issue.

episode_key: 2025-09-17 - Apps make $50K/mo like THIS (I shouldn't share this)
title: Apps make $50K/mo like THIS (I shouldn't share this)
executive_summary: Cody Schneider reveals the creator marketing playbook that consumer apps use to achieve cheaper-than-paid-ads CPMs on TikTok and Instagram. The key distinction: creators (zero followers, paid $900/month for daily posts) outperform influencers ($2K for one post) because for-you-page algorithms reward content quality over follower count. The strategy involves hiring 5-10 creators, testing 30+ videos monthly, sharing winning formats across the team, and converting top organic performers into paid ads.
key_takeaways: Hire creators at $900/month for a post per day across all short-form platforms - at 500K aggregate impressions, you achieve a $2 CPM vs. $6-8 on paid channels
Work with 5-10 creators simultaneously to create a compound learning flywheel: share winning formats across the team and double down on what works
Promote your best-performing creator to team manager to handle logistics, quality control, and format distribution while continuing their own posting
opportunities: Creator management platform specifically for brand-creator relationships (scheduling, format sharing, performance tracking, payment)
Creator-as-a-service offering for vibe-coded consumer apps that need short-form distribution but lack marketing expertise
quotes: If I can pay a dollar and then that turns into $5 in customer lifetime value - that is all ads are when you set it up. It's like that is literally the game here.

episode_key: 2025-09-19 - My AI agents scrape Google Maps to make $$ in BORING businesses
title: My AI agents scrape Google Maps to make $$ in BORING businesses
executive_summary: James, aka The Boring Marketer, demonstrates an n8n workflow that scrapes Google Maps to validate boring local business opportunities. The workflow collects review volume, review velocity, sentiment analysis, competitor density, and social presence for any niche in any city. His thesis: target underserved micro-niches (hardscaping, closet redesign, garage renovation) in tier-2 cities where demand outstrips supply. Rather than starting the service business directly, he advocates building local media assets (newsletters, directories) to generate and sell leads.
key_takeaways: Use Google Maps review data as a market intelligence source: high review velocity plus low average ratings signals strong demand with poor supply - a clear arbitrage opportunity
Target tier-2 cities (Nashville, Charlotte, Denver) where population growth creates service demand that existing providers cannot meet
Build the media company first (local newsletter, directory) to own the customer relationship, then monetize via lead generation at $100-200 per qualified lead
opportunities: AI-powered local niche research tool that automates Google Maps scraping, sentiment analysis, and opportunity scoring for boring business ideas
Lead generation businesses in underserved local service niches (mobile diesel mechanics, smart home installation, exotic car detailing)
quotes: Nobody's really talking about it, which blows my mind. On my timeline, it's literally all the same things - HVAC, plumbing. And what's going to happen is you're going to have a bunch of people who have no business building in that space go into an extremely competitive space.

episode_key: 2025-09-22 - Making $100K/month with iOS apps (BREAKDOWN)
title: Making $100K/month with iOS apps (BREAKDOWN)
executive_summary: Greg Isenberg presents a comprehensive framework for building consumer mobile apps that reach $100K MRR. The playbook covers: selecting a daily habit to serve, validating demand on Reddit/TikTok, prototyping with Lovable/Bolt, distribution testing on one channel with three formats, pricing architecture (7-day free trial, $7-40/month), and scaling via ASO, affiliates, and paid ads. Four specific app ideas are pitched: dog allergy scanner, migraine weather guard, silent study timer, and plant watering AI coach.
key_takeaways: Target daily habits (not weekly/monthly use cases) and ensure there is evidence of paid demand - frequency drives retention and willingness to pay
Achieve the user's first win within the first hour of onboarding; every hour of delay represents an order of magnitude worse retention
Track weekly active paying users (WAPU) as your North Star metric, with LTV/CAC above 3x as the threshold for scaling paid acquisition
opportunities: Niche AI mobile apps that combine photo-scan functionality with personalized tracking (pet allergies, plant care, food safety)
Study productivity apps with gamified streaks, shared virtual rooms, and lo-fi music integration targeting exam prep communities
quotes: People think it's getting saturated with consumer mobile apps, but I don't think that's the case. There's going to be like a hundred mini versions of CalAI or more that are successful.

episode_key: 2025-10-04 - How I'd use Sora 2 + Claude to hit 1M+ views on AI videos
title: How I'd use Sora 2 + Claude to hit 1M+ views on AI videos
executive_summary: Greg Isenberg walks through a three-tool workflow for creating viral short-form video ads: Perplexity Deep Research for audience psychology analysis, Claude for generating and scoring 10 video concepts against engagement criteria, and Sora 2 for producing the final content. The key innovation is using Claude as both a concept generator and evaluation agent, rating each idea on hook strength, pattern interrupt, emotional triggers, and algorithm fit before generating optimized Sora prompts.
key_takeaways: Use Perplexity Deep Research to understand the psychological triggers of your target audience before generating any creative concepts
Have Claude evaluate and score each video concept (1-10) on hook strength, emotional trigger, and algorithm fit before investing in production
Prompt Claude to write Sora-optimized prompts rather than writing them yourself - this leverages Claude's understanding of Sora 2's capabilities for better output
opportunities: AI-powered creative brief generator that combines audience research, concept scoring, and platform-optimized prompt generation into a single workflow
Sora 2 ad creative agency specializing in scroll-stopping content for bootstrapped SaaS and consumer app founders
quotes: The people who are going to write incredible Sora prompts will have the power of Hollywood in their hands.

episode_key: 2025-10-08 - OpenAI's NEW AI Agent Builder Replaces n8n & Zapier
title: OpenAI's NEW AI Agent Builder Replaces n8n & Zapier
executive_summary: Amir demonstrates OpenAI's new Agent Builder, ChatKit SDK, and Widgets - three tools that enable non-technical users to build multi-agent workflows visually and deploy them as website chatbots. The live demo builds a customer classifier that routes inbound inquiries to either a support agent (trained on a vector store) or a sales lead capture agent, with configurable reasoning levels, guard rails for hallucination prevention, and MCP integrations for CRM updates. The episode positions this as a significant democratization of agentic AI.
key_takeaways: OpenAI's Agent Builder lets you visually create multi-agent workflows with parallel/sequential steps, tool calling, guard rails, and MCP integrations - no code required
Use minimal reasoning for simple data collection tasks (sales qualification) and high reasoning for complex problem-solving (support troubleshooting) to optimize speed and cost
ChatKit enables non-engineering teams to deploy and update chatbots on websites without developer dependency - changes publish directly from the Agent Builder
opportunities: Pre-built agent workflow templates for specific verticals (e-commerce support, SaaS onboarding, real estate lead qualification)
Agent Builder consulting services helping SMBs replace expensive chatbot subscriptions with custom OpenAI-powered solutions
quotes: What OpenAI has done with their new update is they've created a visual interface for you to actually build workflows using agents - it's essentially reducing the barrier for non-technical people to get started with building multi-agent workflows.

episode_key: 2025-10-15 - Genspark's Super AI Agent is INSANE
title: Genspark's Super AI Agent is INSANE
executive_summary: Greg Isenberg reviews GenSpark AI, the most-requested hidden gem AI app from his 500K+ X followers. The platform's killer feature is multi-agent mode: one prompt simultaneously queries GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, and Gemini 2.5, then a reflection agent synthesizes the best response. The review covers image generation across multiple models, video creation with auto-prompting, AI slides with fact-checking, and AI sheets for lead research. At ~$20/month, GenSpark positions as a cheaper alternative to running multiple AI subscriptions.
key_takeaways: Multi-agent AI tools that query multiple LLMs simultaneously and reflect on outputs save significant time and token costs compared to manual LLM ping-pong
Auto-prompting features that optimize your inputs for each specific model dramatically improve output quality, especially for image and video generation
Use AI slide tools with fact-checking to create fundraising decks and lead magnets, then export to Google Slides for final polish
opportunities: Niche multi-agent AI platforms specialized for specific workflows (e.g., content creation, market research, investor decks)
Lead magnet automation pipeline: AI generates content, creates slides, exports to landing pages, collects emails
quotes: Instead of me having to go individually to those products, I have it right there. And the other thing that it should be doing is reflecting on what is the best output.

episode_key: 2025-10-20 - Make $1M+ in 2026 with this Trend (IRL is back)
title: Make $1M+ in 2026 with this Trend (IRL is back)
executive_summary: Jonathan Courtney of AJ&Smart argues that the biggest business opportunity in 2026 is the anti-AI trend: in-person experiences. Social media usage peaked in 2022 among young people, creating massive demand for IRL events that feel like an escape from digital overload. Jonathan shares how he sold 30 tickets at $6,800 each for a 'Summer Camp' mastermind and $14,300 certifications that outsell their $6,000 online equivalents. The framework: take popular digital-only products, create themed in-person versions, leverage natural scarcity, and use the community to fund software products.
key_takeaways: In-person events have inherent scarcity that makes marketing dramatically easier than digital products - limited tickets create genuine urgency without artificial FOMO tactics
Price in-person experiences 2-3x higher than digital equivalents and lead with the human connection value proposition - people are craving real-world interaction
Use IRL events and communities as the top of funnel to build relationships, then vibe code SaaS products and sell them to the community you've already built
opportunities: Premium in-person mastermind events in any niche where digital-only communities currently dominate (marketing, AI, investing, health)
IRL community-funded SaaS model: build the community first through events, validate product ideas in person, then sell software to members
quotes: Scarcity is really hard to do with a digital product. In-person events actually have a limited amount of tickets. So it's way easier to create a true sense of urgency and scarcity.

episode_key: 2025-10-27 - Claude Skills Built Me an AI Agent Army (They Run Everything Now)
title: Claude Skills Built Me an AI Agent Army (They Run Everything Now)
executive_summary: Amir explains Claude Skills - a feature that creates reusable, laser-focused AI workflows with custom instructions, reference files, and executable scripts. Unlike projects (broad workspaces) or sub-agents (conversation-scoped), skills load context only when relevant, reducing hallucination from context overload. Live demos include building a UTM link generator artifact, an A/B testing analyzer, and a marketing insights agent. The episode argues that skills solve the #1 complaint about enterprise AI adoption: inconsistent and incorrect output.
key_takeaways: Claude Skills solve context rot by loading only task-relevant context rather than overwhelming the model with everything - think 'right amount of context' not 'all the context'
Create custom scripts within skills for deterministic data analysis (specific formulas, column operations) rather than relying on the LLM's non-deterministic interpretation
Skills can be sold as products - there is an emerging marketplace for specialized Claude skills and plugins that others can install for their workflows
opportunities: Marketplace for industry-specific Claude Skills (paid media analysis, financial reporting, legal document review)
Claude Skills consulting service that audits enterprise workflows and builds custom skill libraries for go-to-market teams
quotes: AI is essentially your coworker or someone that reports to you. You want to train it. You want to build the guidelines and limit the constraints around it.

episode_key: 2025-11-10 - Glif AI: The $10 App That Replaces a Full Creative Team
title: Glif AI: The $10 App That Replaces a Full Creative Team
executive_summary: Greg Isenberg explores Glif AI, a $10 creative AI platform that chains multiple models (Nano Banana, Kling, Wan 2.2, ElevenLabs) into agent workflows for non-technical users. Live demos include redesigning YouTube thumbnails in Mr. Beast style, creating 40-second miniature history documentaries with AI-generated images, voiceover, and music, and building AI influencer talking-head videos. The broader thesis: brands should create 10 creator accounts (mix of real and AI) rather than one brand account, using tools like Glif to scale content production.
key_takeaways: The missing step in creative AI is prompt optimization - agent workflows that analyze your input and generate model-specific prompts dramatically improve output quality
Build multiple creator accounts (real + AI influencers) per brand rather than one brand account - if even 1-2 of 10 accounts pop, you gain massive distribution
Follow the ACP funnel: Audience first (creative AI content), then Community (paid groups, IRL events), then Product (vibe code and sell to your audience)
opportunities: AI influencer creation agency that builds and manages multiple AI personality accounts for brands across Instagram and TikTok
Creative AI workflow marketplace where experts sell optimized multi-model pipelines for specific content types (thumbnails, ads, documentaries)
quotes: The people that could unleash creative AI have an unfair advantage because those are the people that are going to be able to create big audiences, ads that outperform, and vibe-coded websites that don't look like every other vibe-coded app.

episode_key: 2025-11-13 - Startup idea someone should steal
title: Startup idea someone should steal
executive_summary: Greg Isenberg presents a new format covering one startup idea (female solo travel safety review platform), one trend (gamification in apps), one news analysis (ex-Reddit CEO's AI bear thesis rebuttal), one growth framework (creator + affiliate + gamification), and one AI tool (Crea AI). The standout idea is a map-based platform where verified female travelers rate neighborhoods on safety metrics, monetized via subscriptions and hotel safety certifications. Greg argues against the thesis that foundation model companies will crush all AI application startups.
key_takeaways: Build a value ladder for your startup: free quiz (email capture) to community access ($10/mo) to premium subscription ($30/mo) to B2B certification ($500/mo) - each step increases trust and price
Gamification is an underrated UI trend across apps and websites - there is an opportunity for a specialist agency focused solely on adding gamification layers to existing products
Use a 3-step growth framework: attach the right creator (shoot your shot with 100, focus on sub-120K followers), offer 30-50% lifetime affiliate commissions, and gamify the affiliate experience
opportunities: Safety-focused travel platform for women with map-based reviews, hotel certifications, and community features - 84% of solo female travelers report feeling unsafe
Gamification consulting agency that specializes in adding points, streaks, leaderboards, and challenges to SaaS and consumer apps
quotes: AI application startups that actually own the workflow, own the customer, and have network effects are going to be extremely valuable in the future.

episode_key: 2025-12-03 - 10 Unknown Apps Making $50K+ MRR (Copy Them)
title: 10 Unknown Apps Making $50K+ MRR (Copy Them)
executive_summary: Greg Isenberg reverse-engineers eight mobile apps making $50K+ MRR in the last 30 days, extracting replicable patterns: AI video generators (FlashLoop), religious note-taking apps, AI interior design tools, emoji/sticker creators, vinyl record scanners, multi-LLM assistants, logo makers, and healthy restaurant menu finders. Each breakdown covers why it works psychologically, its pricing model, and 3-5 adjacent niches where the same format could be applied. The episode concludes with six frameworks for ideating mobile app ideas.
key_takeaways: Apps succeeding at $50K+ MRR share common traits: they serve daily habits, have simple one-feature cores, leverage AI for personalization, and use ASO-optimized names
The most profitable mobile app niches combine identity expression (vanity), financial stakes (collectibles), or health anxiety (allergies, calories) with high-frequency usage patterns
Simple names dominate ASO: 'Logo Maker' does $200K/month because people literally search 'logo maker' in the App Store - name your app what people search for
opportunities: AI-powered collectibles scanner apps for specific verticals (comic books, sports cards, vintage clothing) with instant valuation and marketplace integration
Niche AI assistant apps that bundle multiple LLMs for specific professional workflows (student research, sales CRM, legal document analysis)
quotes: Now is the greatest time to be building mobile apps. And there are people printing doing it.

episode_key: 2025-12-05 - With Harvey Co-Founder Gabe Pereyra
title: With Harvey Co-Founder Gabe Pereyra
executive_summary: Harvey co-founder Gabe Pereyra discusses building AI for the legal industry, now approaching 1,000 customers and 500 employees. The conversation covers Harvey's evolution from individual lawyer productivity to firm-wide organizational transformation, the analogy between legal reasoning traces and coding RL environments, and why Harvey chose to build a platform for all law firms rather than compete as a law firm itself. Pereyra draws parallels between distinguished engineers and senior law partners, emphasizing that the real value lies in decade-long pattern recognition that current models cannot replicate.
key_takeaways: The shift from individual productivity tools to organizational productivity platforms represents the next frontier - making a lawyer 20% faster does not make a law firm 20% more productive
Legal RL environments using partner feedback and internal editing history as reward signals offer a promising path for training domain-specific reasoning models
Forward-deployed engineering teams are essential for large enterprise AI adoption - the value is not just the product but helping firms transform their business operations
opportunities: Professional services collaboration platforms that enable secure data sharing and AI-powered workflows across law firms, investment banks, and consultancies
RL training data businesses that capture expert decision-making processes (reasoning traces) in knowledge-intensive professions
quotes: When we first got access to GPT-4, Winston went in his room for 14 hours and just redid a bunch of his associate tasks. And what I saw was essentially hacky auto-agentic work.

episode_key: 2025-12-11 - With ElevenLabs Co-Founder Mati Staniszewski
title: With ElevenLabs Co-Founder Mati Staniszewski
executive_summary: ElevenLabs co-founder Mati Staniszewski discusses the company's growth to $300M ARR and 350 employees, split roughly 50/50 between self-serve creative tools and enterprise agent platforms. Key themes include voice as the future interface for all devices and robots, the architectural advantage of cascaded vs. fused speech models, and why ElevenLabs' research moat comes from 10 of the world's top 50-100 audio researchers rather than compute scale. Notable deployments include Epic Games (Darth Vader in Fortnite), chess.com (learn from Magnus Carlsen), MasterClass (practice negotiations with Chris Voss), and Ukraine's agentic government initiative.
key_takeaways: Voice AI is shifting from reactive customer support to proactive customer experience - the biggest opportunity is making voice agents the front door of e-commerce and service discovery

Research advantages in audio AI come from architectural breakthroughs by a small number of elite researchers, not compute scale - but models will eventually commoditize, making the product layer essential
Education will be the largest voice AI use case: personalized AI tutors that understand individual learners, delivered through headphones, with the voices of iconic teachers
opportunities: Voice-first e-commerce experiences where AI agents guide customers from product discovery through checkout using personalized voice interactions
AI-powered education platforms with celebrity/expert voice clones for personalized tutoring across languages
quotes: In the ideal world in the future you will be able to have content delivered naturally around languages still carrying the original voice, original emotions, original intonation.

episode_key: 2025-12-19 - The 2026 AI Forecast with Sarah and Elad
title: The 2026 AI Forecast with Sarah and Elad
executive_summary: Sarah Guo and Elad Gil deliver their 2026 AI predictions with guest contributions from industry leaders. Key forecasts: next wave of vertical AI consolidation (beyond coding/legal/medical), digital biology's ChatGPT moment for protein generation, robotics hype cycle correction, more AI IPOs with strong retail demand, breakthrough consumer AI experiences, and defense tech acceleration. The episode pushes back on overstated bubble narratives, noting that the slowest technology adopters (doctors, lawyers) are actually the fastest AI adopters. Guest predictions cover proactive AI agents, context as the key product differentiator, and the year YOLO dies.
key_takeaways: The next set of vertical AI companies will consolidate in 2026 - look for the domain equivalent of what Cursor did for coding in industries like accounting, nursing, and compliance
Digital biology is approaching its generative AI moment: protein generation, chemical understanding, and cell foundation models will create breakthrough applications
Defense tech is accelerating structurally, not just as hype - the shift to autonomous drone-based warfare and the need for domestic supply chains creates lasting demand
opportunities: Vertical AI applications in professional domains with severe labor shortages (accounting, nursing, compliance, insurance)
AI-driven materials science and drug discovery companies leveraging new foundation models for molecular design
quotes: The people who tended to be the slowest adopters of technology love AI. Physicians, lawyers, certain accounting types - it's actually kind of fascinating.

episode_key: 2026-01-05 - Watch me use AI to make millions in ecommerce
title: Watch me use AI to make millions in ecommerce
executive_summary: Greg Isenberg reviews Alibaba's Axio AI agent platform, which combines trend analysis, product design generation, supplier matching, and margin calculation into a single e-commerce research workflow. The demo covers identifying rising trends in baby products, generating Squid Game merchandise concepts with AI-designed mockups, finding verified manufacturers with pricing, and analyzing unmet customer pain points in mechanical keyboards using Amazon reviews and Reddit forums. The platform's agentic approach automatically researches willingness-to-pay signals before delivering recommendations.
key_takeaways: Use AI agent platforms like Axio to compress the e-commerce research cycle from weeks to hours - trend identification, product design, supplier matching, and margin analysis in one workflow
Mine Amazon review sentiment to find product categories with high review velocity but low average ratings (3.8 or below) - this signals strong demand with poor supply quality
Look for willingness-to-pay signals in Reddit forums and Amazon Q&A where customers explicitly state 'I'd pay extra for X feature' to validate premium positioning
opportunities: AI-powered e-commerce research tools that combine trend data, competitor analysis, pain point extraction, and supplier matching for aspiring product entrepreneurs
Senior pet products (orthopedic beds, cognitive stimulation toys, joint supplements) based on aging pet demographic with underserved market quality
quotes: This isn't just an AI agent platform. This is a smart AI agent platform specifically designed with e-commerce in mind.

episode_key: 2026-01-08 - NVIDIAs Jensen Huang on Reasoning Models Robotics and AI Bubble
title: NVIDIAs Jensen Huang on Reasoning Models Robotics and AI Bubble
executive_summary: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang delivers a wide-ranging year-end interview covering AI's five-layer technology stack, the critical distinction between job tasks and job purpose, the importance of open source to American AI leadership, and why the AI bubble narrative is overly simplistic. Huang predicts digital biology's ChatGPT moment, reasoning-powered breakthroughs in self-driving and robotics, and a billion-fold reduction in token generation costs over 10 years. He firmly pushes back on doomer narratives, arguing that technology advancement is the primary driver of AI safety.
key_takeaways: Distinguish between job tasks and job purpose: radiologists increased despite 100% AI adoption because AI automated the task (reading scans) while expanding the purpose (diagnosing disease, doing research)
The cost of token generation will decrease roughly a billion times over the next decade through compounding hardware improvements (5-10x/year), algorithm advances, and architectural innovation
Open source AI is essential infrastructure that benefits education, startups, research, and entire industries - policy decisions must protect this innovation flywheel
opportunities: Digital biology applications (protein generation, chemical understanding, cell foundation models) approaching a breakthrough moment enabled by synthetic data and world foundation models
Vertical AI specialization: as models diversify, startups can achieve superhuman performance in narrow domains without needing to be great at everything
quotes: Deep Seek was probably the single most important paper that most Silicon Valley researchers read. Probably the single greatest contribution to American AI last year.

episode_key: 2026-01-15 - The Largest Untapped Market Inside US Defense Emil Michael
title: The Largest Untapped Market Inside US Defense Emil Michael
executive_summary: Emil Michael, Under Secretary for Research & Engineering at the Department of War, outlines the largest technology transformation in US defense history. Overseeing DARPA, the Chief AI Office, and Defense Innovation Unit, he has consolidated 14 critical technology areas into 6 actionable priorities: applied AI, scaled hypersonics, directed energy, autonomous systems, and supply chain resilience. GenAI.mil reached 1M unique users in 30 days across all 3M department employees. The episode details how entrepreneurs can now enter defense through the front door rather than suing for contracts.
key_takeaways: The Department of War is shifting from 300-page RFP questionnaires (which favor incumbents) to problem-statement-based procurement that allows startups to propose novel solutions
GenAI.mil deployed to 3 million employees in 60 days with 1 million monthly active users - proving enterprise AI adoption can happen at unprecedented speed with the right talent and urgency

The defense industrial base needs new entrants across the full supply chain: from germanium and brushless motors to fiber optic cables - the Office of Strategic Capital has $200B in lending authority for these gaps
opportunities: Defense-focused AI application companies solving enterprise, intelligence fusion, and wargaming use cases for the Department of War
Critical minerals and component manufacturing businesses addressing supply chain dependencies currently outsourced to adversarial nations
quotes: It's crazy to me that SpaceX and Anduril and Palantir all had to sue the Department of War for their first contract. So the idea is you don't have to sue anymore. Come through the front door.

episode_key: 2026-01-16 - I Spent $289 So AI Could Build My Business
title: I Spent $289 So AI Could Build My Business
executive_summary: Serial entrepreneur Samuel Thompson demonstrates how to launch an AI-generated info product business in under an hour using ChatGPT, Shopify, Canva, Fiverr, and Envato Elements. His case study: a divorce guide ebook written entirely by ChatGPT that evolved from a messy first version to a 197-page book with case studies, selling via Facebook ads at near break-even CAC. The episode covers the full stack: AI book generation, cover design in Canva, product mockups via Envato, Shopify store setup with conversion-optimized themes (Solo Drop, Elixir), and scaling with white-label supplements.
key_takeaways: Generate a minimum viable info product with ChatGPT in under an hour, validate demand with a simple landing page and small ad spend, then iterate the content quality only after proving sales
Use conversion-optimized Shopify themes (Solo Drop at $200 lifetime, Elixir at $175) that include built-in urgency triggers, multi-tier discounts, and mobile optimization
White-label suppliers (Supptel for supplements, Anox for skincare) let you test product-market fit with zero inventory risk before committing to a manufacturer for scale
opportunities: AI-generated niche info products in high-emotion categories (divorce, health conditions, pet care) sold via Facebook ads with rapid CAC payback

quotes: A guy on Amazon has done $30 million selling sugar as hummingbird nectar over the last 10 years and his branding is atrocious. Has never run a Facebook ad in his life.

episode_key: 2026-01-22 - No Priors Live Is the SaaS Bear Thesis Overblown MongoDB CEO
title: No Priors Live Is the SaaS Bear Thesis Overblown MongoDB CEO
executive_summary: MongoDB CEO Dev Ittycheria (CJ Desai) pushes back on the dominant SaaS bear thesis in a live No Priors recording. His core argument: platforms are sticky, products are not. Only single-digit companies have crossed $10B in pure-play software revenue because building a true platform (multiple integrated products, deep system integrations, enterprise-grade compliance) is exceptionally rare. He argues that vibe-coded apps increase application velocity but don't solve enterprise requirements like multi-cloud resiliency, regulatory compliance, and air-gapped networks. MongoDB's structural advantage: unstructured data storage is the one constant in the AI stack.
key_takeaways: Platforms are sticky, products are not - enterprise customers with 300+ applications built on your platform become structurally locked in, creating durable competitive advantage
The SaaS bear thesis is overblown: while individual products can be replaced, the data layer and LLM layer will always be required in any AI application stack
To prove investors wrong on the bear thesis, enterprise software companies must demonstrate reacceleration of growth through AI - innovating more must translate directly to selling more
opportunities: AI-native SaaS companies that position as full system-of-record replacements (not add-on layers) with disruptive pricing and faster speed-to-value than incumbents
Vertical AI applications built on MongoDB's unstructured data capabilities for industries drowning in messy, multi-format data (insurance claims, healthcare records)
quotes: Products can be replaced. Platforms are rare. And platforms are rare. So your dream or aspiration as a software company should be that you become a platform.

The intersection of biology and engineering approaches to preservation offers breakthrough potential that neither field could achieve alone
Reducing time pressure in organ transplants enables better donor-recipient matching and reduces the 20%+ waste rate of viable organs

key_takeaways: AI agents can compress digital forensics investigation timelines from days to hours by automating log analysis, pattern recognition, and attack chain reconstruction

episode_key: 2026-02-13 - Approaching the AI Event Horizon Part 1

executive_summary: The Cognitive Revolution examines the concept of an AI 'event horizon' - the point at which AI capabilities advance so rapidly that predicting outcomes becomes impossible. The discussion covers the acceleration of AI research, the compression of development timelines, and the implications for governance, safety, and society. The episode argues that we are approaching a period where AI progress compounds faster than our ability to adapt institutional frameworks.
key_takeaways: AI development timelines are compressing faster than institutional adaptation - what took years now takes months, creating governance gaps that need urgent attention

Preparedness requires investing in AI safety and alignment research at the same pace as capabilities research, not as an afterthought
opportunities: AI governance consulting firms helping organizations prepare institutional frameworks for rapidly advancing AI capabilities

quotes: We are approaching a point where AI progress compounds faster than our ability to adapt the frameworks we use to understand and govern it.

episode_key: 2026-02-18 - Mathematical Superintelligence Harmonics Vlad and Tudor on IMO Gold

episode_key: 2026-02-19 - The AI Code Slop Risk or Opportunity
title: The AI Code Slop Risk or Opportunity
executive_summary: No Priors examines the dual-edged phenomenon of AI-generated code proliferation: while AI coding tools have dramatically increased developer productivity, they have also created a flood of low-quality 'code slop' that creates maintenance debt and security vulnerabilities. The discussion weighs whether this represents a net risk or opportunity, concluding that the companies building AI-powered code quality, security scanning, and automated testing tools will capture enormous value.
key_takeaways: AI-generated code volume is growing faster than the ability to review it, creating a systemic quality and security risk that requires AI-powered solutions
The opportunity lies in building the 'immune system' for AI-generated code: automated testing, security scanning, and quality assurance tools that scale with generation speed
Companies that help organizations manage the transition from human-written to AI-assisted codebases will capture significant enterprise value
opportunities: AI-powered code quality and security platforms that can review AI-generated code at the speed it is produced
Automated testing and continuous verification tools specifically designed for AI-assisted development workflows
quotes: The question is not whether AI code slop is a problem - it is. The question is whether you build the tools to detect and fix it, or let it accumulate as technical debt.

episode_key: 2026-02-25 - Max Welling Materials Underlie Everything
title: Max Welling Materials Underlie Everything
executive_summary: AI researcher Max Welling discusses how machine learning is revolutionizing materials science, arguing that materials are the foundation of every technological breakthrough from semiconductors to batteries to pharmaceuticals. The conversation covers generative models for molecular design, the challenges of sparse experimental data in materials, and why materials science AI requires fundamentally different approaches than language or vision models.
key_takeaways: Materials science AI requires different architectures than language models because molecular structures have geometric and physical constraints that text does not
Generative models for materials design can explore chemical spaces that would take decades of traditional experimentation to search
The bottleneck in materials AI is not compute but high-quality experimental data - partnerships with labs and automated experimentation are critical
opportunities: AI-driven materials discovery platforms for battery chemistry, semiconductor materials, and sustainable manufacturing
Automated experimentation and high-throughput screening systems that generate training data for materials AI models
quotes: Materials underlie everything - every technological breakthrough ultimately depends on discovering the right material for the job.

episode_key: 2026-02-26 - Whos Actually Funding the AI Buildout
title: Whos Actually Funding the AI Buildout
executive_summary: No Priors examines the capital structure behind the massive AI infrastructure buildout, tracing the flow of money from sovereign wealth funds and pension funds through hyperscalers to chip manufacturers and data center developers. The episode analyzes the systemic risks of concentrated capital deployment, the credit agreements underpinning data center construction, and whether the demand for AI compute justifies the unprecedented investment levels.
key_takeaways: The AI infrastructure buildout is funded through a complex chain of capital: sovereign wealth funds and pension funds to hyperscalers to chip companies to data center REITs, each with different risk profiles
The biggest systemic risk is not whether AI demand is real, but whether the credit agreements and pay-on-delivery contracts create a chain of dependency that could cascade if any link weakens
Energy availability, not capital, is becoming the binding constraint on AI infrastructure deployment - natural gas is the only practical solution for the next decade
opportunities: Energy infrastructure companies focused on powering AI data centers, particularly natural gas and nuclear solutions
AI infrastructure financing and credit analysis tools for investors evaluating the risk-return profile of the compute buildout
quotes: The question investors are really asking is not whether AI demand is real - it's who is actually holding the risk in this massive capital chain.

episode_key: 2026-02-26 - Dylan Patel Explains the AI War While Cooking
title: Dylan Patel Explains the AI War While Cooking
executive_summary: SemiAnalysis founder Dylan Patel breaks down the semiconductor and AI infrastructure competitive landscape in an informal cooking-format interview. Topics include the GPU supply chain, NVIDIA's architectural moat, the China chip war dynamics, custom silicon efforts by hyperscalers, and the economics of inference vs. training compute. Patel provides granular analysis of chip economics, data center power constraints, and why the hardware landscape is more complex than most software-focused investors understand.
key_takeaways: NVIDIA's moat is not just performance but the CUDA ecosystem and architectural compatibility that ensures every algorithm optimization runs across their entire installed base
Custom silicon from hyperscalers (Google TPUs, Amazon Trainium) is competitive for specific inference workloads but cannot match NVIDIA's flexibility for rapidly evolving model architectures
The semiconductor supply chain has critical single points of failure (TSMC, specific chemical suppliers) that represent geopolitical risk factors beyond just US-China trade tensions
opportunities: Alternative compute infrastructure companies that can deliver competitive inference performance at lower cost for specific model architectures
Supply chain diversification businesses addressing critical dependencies in the semiconductor manufacturing pipeline
quotes: The AI war is really a semiconductor war, and understanding the full supply chain from chemicals to packaging to architecture is essential to understanding who wins.

Research-as-a-service offerings that combine AI search with computer use to deliver complete competitive intelligence or market analysis reports

episode_key: 2026-03-05 - Dont Fight Backprop Goodfires Vision for Intentional Design
title: Dont Fight Backprop Goodfires Vision for Intentional Design
executive_summary: Goodfire's founders discuss their vision for intentional AI design through mechanistic interpretability - understanding and controlling what happens inside neural networks rather than just tuning inputs and outputs. The conversation covers how interpretability tools can enable more precise model behavior control, reduce hallucinations, and create AI systems that behave more predictably and safely.
key_takeaways: Mechanistic interpretability allows engineers to understand and control model behavior at a granular level, going beyond prompt engineering to actual neural circuit-level interventions
The philosophy of 'don't fight backprop' means working with the model's learned representations rather than against them through post-hoc constraints
Interpretability tools will become essential for enterprise AI deployment where predictable, auditable behavior is a regulatory requirement
opportunities: Interpretability-as-a-service platforms that help enterprises understand and control their AI model behavior for compliance and safety
Model editing and steering tools that allow fine-grained behavior modification without full retraining
quotes: Don't fight backprop - work with the model's learned representations to achieve intentional design rather than trying to constrain behavior from the outside.

key_takeaways: Cloud agents represent a paradigm shift from AI-assisted coding (suggestions in your IDE) to AI-executed coding (agents that independently build features in cloud environments)

The future of software development will involve humans defining specifications and reviewing agent-produced code rather than writing it line by line

Specification-to-code platforms where product managers describe features in natural language and AI agents implement them end-to-end

episode_key: 2026-03-09 - OpenClaws Memory Sucks and the fix is simple
title: OpenClaws Memory Sucks and the fix is simple
executive_summary: Supermemory founder Dhravya Shah diagnoses the memory problem in AI coding agents like OpenClaw, arguing that current memory systems are too simplistic (append-only logs) to support effective long-running agent sessions. The proposed fix involves structured memory with hierarchical organization, relevance scoring, and automatic pruning - drawing from how human memory works with short-term, working, and long-term memory systems.
key_takeaways: Current AI agent memory systems (simple append-only context) degrade performance over long sessions as irrelevant information crowds out important context
Effective agent memory requires hierarchical structure: short-term (current task), working (active project), and long-term (organizational knowledge) with automatic relevance scoring
Memory management is becoming a critical infrastructure layer for AI agents - the agents that remember effectively will dramatically outperform those that don't
opportunities: Memory infrastructure for AI agents that provides hierarchical, structured context management with automatic relevance scoring and pruning
Agent memory-as-a-service platforms that any AI agent framework can plug into for persistent, intelligent context management
quotes: OpenClaw's memory sucks and the fix is simple - stop treating memory as an append-only log and start treating it like human memory with structure and relevance.

episode_key: 2026-03-09 - I gave OpenClaw one job go viral (it worked)

The episode documents the agent's autonomous research into trending topics, content format analysis, hook optimization, and multi-platform distribution strategy. key_takeaways: AI agents given a clear success metric (virality) can autonomously research trends, create optimized content, and develop distribution strategies across platforms
The key to effective agent tasking is defining the outcome metric clearly rather than specifying the process - let the agent figure out the how
Current AI agents are most effective for content tasks where success is measurable (views, engagement) versus subjective quality assessments
opportunities: AI agent-powered content marketing services where clients define success metrics and agents autonomously execute the full creation-to-distribution pipeline
Agent performance benchmarking platforms that compare different AI agents on real-world content creation and marketing tasks

The episode explores the implications for knowledge workers, researchers, and entrepreneurs who can now leverage AI to conduct deep research in hours rather than weeks.
key_takeaways: Autonomous research agents that can navigate the web, synthesize multiple sources, and generate structured reports represent a paradigm shift for knowledge work
The democratization of deep research capabilities means that solo founders and small teams can now compete with large organizations on insight quality
The key differentiator is not access to research tools but the quality of questions asked and the ability to act on research findings quickly

Research agent platforms that allow non-technical users to define research objectives and receive comprehensive, cited reports

episode_key: 2026-03-11 - Bioinfohazards Jassi Pannu on Controlling Dangerous Data
title: Bioinfohazards Jassi Pannu on Controlling Dangerous Data
executive_summary: Jassi Pannu discusses the challenge of bioinfohazards - dangerous biological information that could be misused if widely accessible. The conversation covers the tension between open science and biosecurity, how AI models trained on biological data could lower barriers to creating dangerous pathogens, and what governance frameworks are needed to manage information hazards in the age of AI-powered biology.
key_takeaways: AI models trained on biological data could inadvertently lower the knowledge barriers for creating dangerous biological agents, requiring new information governance frameworks
The tension between open science (which accelerates beneficial research) and biosecurity (which requires restricting dangerous knowledge) has no easy resolution
Practical governance approaches include structured access models, responsible disclosure frameworks, and AI model evaluations specifically testing for dual-use biological capabilities
opportunities: Biosecurity evaluation platforms that test AI models for dangerous biological knowledge and capabilities before deployment
Structured access systems for sensitive biological databases that enable legitimate research while preventing misuse
quotes: The challenge with bioinfohazards is that the same information that enables life-saving research can also enable catastrophic misuse.

episode_key: 2026-03-12 - From Note-Taking App to AI Workspace The Simon Last Interview

The conversation covers how Notion is integrating AI across its product suite, the strategic decision to build AI features natively versus partnering with LLM providers, and the competitive dynamics of the productivity software market as AI transforms how teams organize knowledge and collaborate.
key_takeaways: The transition from note-taking to AI workspace requires rethinking the product around AI-native interactions rather than bolting AI onto existing features
Notion's advantage is its structured knowledge base that provides rich context for AI - the quality of AI outputs is directly proportional to the quality of organized information it can access
The productivity software market is being restructured by AI: tools that can serve as both the knowledge layer and the AI interaction layer will capture the most value
opportunities: AI-native workspace platforms that combine knowledge management, task automation, and AI interaction in a unified interface
Enterprise knowledge graph solutions that structure organizational information to maximize the quality of AI-powered workflows
quotes: The future of productivity tools is not AI as a feature - it's the entire workspace reimagined around AI as the primary way you interact with your team's knowledge.

episode_key: 2026-03-14 - Monty the ultrafast Python interpreter by Agents for Agents
title: Monty the ultrafast Python interpreter by Agents for Agents
executive_summary: Latent Space covers Monty, an ultrafast Python interpreter specifically designed for AI agents rather than human developers. The episode explores why existing Python interpreters are too slow for agentic workflows that need to execute thousands of code snippets rapidly, how Monty optimizes for the specific patterns of AI-generated code, and the emerging infrastructure layer of tools built by agents for agents.
key_takeaways: AI agents generate and execute code differently than humans - they need interpreters optimized for rapid execution of many small snippets rather than long-running programs
The 'tools for agents' infrastructure layer is an emerging category: agents need specialized runtimes, sandboxes, and execution environments different from human developer tools
Performance optimization at the interpreter level has multiplicative effects on agent capabilities since faster code execution enables more iterations and more complex reasoning chains
opportunities: Agent-optimized runtime environments and sandboxes that provide fast, secure code execution for AI coding agents
Infrastructure tooling specifically designed for AI agent workflows rather than human developer workflows
quotes: Monty is built by agents for agents - the realization that AI agents need fundamentally different tools than human developers is creating an entire new infrastructure layer.

episode_key: 2026-03-16 - AI Scouting Report the Good Bad and Weird at Law and AI
title: AI Scouting Report the Good Bad and Weird at Law and AI
executive_summary: The Cognitive Revolution provides a scouting report on AI adoption in the legal industry, covering what is working well, what is failing, and what unexpected developments have emerged. The analysis examines specific use cases from document review to contract drafting, the regulatory landscape for AI in legal practice, and how law firms are reorganizing around AI capabilities. The episode highlights the gap between vendor promises and practical deployment reality.
key_takeaways: AI excels at legal document review and research but still struggles with nuanced legal reasoning and strategy - the best results come from human-AI collaboration rather than full automation
The biggest barrier to legal AI adoption is not technology but change management - law firms that invest in training and workflow redesign see dramatically better ROI
Unexpected developments include AI-assisted pro bono work expanding access to justice and AI tools enabling solo practitioners to compete with larger firms on complex cases
opportunities: Legal AI training and change management consulting for law firms that have purchased tools but are not seeing expected ROI
AI-powered legal aid platforms that democratize access to quality legal assistance for underserved populations
quotes: The good, bad, and weird of legal AI comes down to this: the technology works better than skeptics claim but worse than vendors promise.

episode_key: 2026-03-17 - Why Anthropic Thinks AI Should Have Its Own Computer
title: Why Anthropic Thinks AI Should Have Its Own Computer
executive_summary: Latent Space explores Anthropic's vision for giving AI its own computer - a dedicated computational environment where AI agents can browse the web, use applications, write and execute code, and perform multi-step tasks autonomously. The discussion covers the technical architecture, security implications, and product design decisions behind Anthropic's computer use capabilities, and what this means for the future of AI agents that can interact with the digital world like humans do.
key_takeaways: Giving AI its own computer environment enables a fundamentally different class of tasks: multi-step workflows that require navigating applications, filling forms, and interacting with web services
The security architecture for AI computer use requires sandboxing, permission systems, and audit trails that don't exist in traditional computing environments
Computer use capabilities transform AI from a text-in/text-out system to a general-purpose digital worker that can automate any workflow a human can perform on a computer
opportunities: Secure sandboxed environments optimized for AI computer use with built-in permissions, monitoring, and rollback capabilities
Workflow automation platforms that leverage computer use APIs to automate any process that currently requires a human clicking through applications
quotes: Giving AI its own computer is not just a feature - it's a paradigm shift that transforms AI from an advisor into a digital worker that can actually do things.
